doom.driver: WHO declares Ebola outbreak in DR Congo a global health emergency. The agency added that the outbreak, with around 246 cases and 80 deaths, does not meet the criteria of pandemic emergency.
doom.blurb: EBOLA GETS THE OFFICIAL SCARY STAMP
doom.summary: The WHO has upgraded the DRC Ebola outbreak to a global health emergency — the highest alarm bell short of 'pandemic emergency,' which is apparently a different, even scarier bell. With 246 cases, 80 deaths, and a strain that has no known vaccine, this is the outbreak that crossed the border from last week's local tragedy into international bureaucratic panic. Meanwhile hantavirus is making cruise ships interesting, and rivers are running out of oxygen — it's giving 'nature's revenge arc.'

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: DeepSeek-V4-Flash reignites interest in LLM steering vectors, but no alarming capability jumps today.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: Trump ousts another dissenting Republican senator, consolidating political loyalty at the cost of institutional checks.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: Taiwan pushes back on Trump's post-Beijing warnings, while Hormuz shipping concerns keep oil stockpile anxiety elevated.

factors.name: Alignment
factors.detail: Musk vs. Altman trial enters final week — entertaining but no systemic AI risk signals.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: WHO formal PHEIC declaration on Bundibugyo Ebola strain with no vaccine, plus hantavirus on a cruise ship — two novel outbreak vectors active simultaneously.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing — weather-dominated exterior scene, heavy equatorial downpour filtering everything through diagonal sheets of rain, camera positioned at mid-height looking across a red laterite dirt road in Bunia, Ituri Province, northeastern DR Congo, deep navy sky bruised with storm clouds pressing low over corrugated iron clinic rooftops and banana palms bending under the weight of rain — in the foreground a white WHO-marked Toyota Land Cruiser sits half-submerged in a rust-red puddle, its blue roundel logo barely visible through the rain-streaked windshield, headlights on and cutting orange cones through the downpour — two figures in full white Tyvek PPE suits with yellow rubber boots and full-face respirator masks move through the mud toward an inflatable orange isolation tent, its biohazard tape strung between wooden stakes sagging under pooled rainwater — a portable chlorine spray canister sits abandoned on a wooden bench under a clinic awning, rain splashing off its metal cylinder — deep shadows dominate every overhang and doorway, the equatorial green of palm fronds turned to dark teal under the storm light — smoldering orange bleeds only from the clinic tent seams and headlights, all other warmth extinguished by rain and navy shadow, atmosphere oppressive and catastrophic.

dawn.driver: North Korean footballers arrive in Seoul for first time in eight years. Naegohyang FC due to play Suwon FC in semi-final of Asian Women's Champions League on Wednesday
dawn.blurb: RIVALS MEET ON THE PITCH, NOT THE BATTLEFIELD
dawn.summary: In a world where geopolitical walls feel higher every day, North Korean women's footballers crossing into South Korea for the first time in eight years is quietly extraordinary. Sport did what diplomacy rarely manages: it moved people across one of the world's most fortified borders under a banner of fair play. Against a backdrop of Ebola emergencies, Hormuz tensions, and missile tests, this small act of athletic cooperation is a reminder that human beings consistently find ways to reach each other.

factors.name: Peace
factors.detail: North and South Korea sharing a stadium for a women's football semi-final is the kind of quiet diplomacy that history books later call a turning point.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: The players themselves — young women who trained hard and simply want to compete — are doing what politicians could not, crossing a border through sheer athletic merit.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: Scientists reversed memory loss by recharging malfunctioning mitochondria, a potential breakthrough for neurodegenerative diseases that affect millions worldwide.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: A first-ever direct image of the cosmic web reveals the universe's hidden structure, expanding our sense of context and shared cosmic home.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Bulgaria's first-ever Eurovision win, powered by an unknown performer and a crowd-storming anthem, is a small, joyful proof that underdogs still triumph.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Extreme close-up filling the entire frame with a single football pressed against a chain-link fence, the ball's black-and-white hexagonal panels catching deep golden amber sidelight from the right, long shadows striping across the leather surface, the fence links rendered in warm teal pixel lines casting a crosshatch shadow lattice across the ball's face, one hexagonal panel sitting precisely over a gap in the fence where the mesh has been pushed open wide enough for the ball to pass through, the gap edges bent back and glowing with warm backlit gold as if light itself is flooding through the breach, the ground below implied by a thin strip of vivid green pixel grass at the frame's lower edge, the fence receding into soft teal shadow at the frame's periphery, the football half-through the gap, mid-crossing, suspended in the act of passage, radiant amber and deep teal, side-lit, long shadows, no flags, no figures, just the universal object moving through the opened threshold.
